imx8mp-var-dart.name: Variscite DART-MX8M-PLUS
imx8mp-var-dart.description: The Variscite DART-MX8M-PLUS is an industrial i.MX 8M Plus SoM. It provisions to an SD card or to onboard eMMC over USB (UUU), selected with the carrier's SW7 boot switch.

prerequisites: Variscite DART-MX8M-PLUS SoM on a Variscite carrier board

options.label: SD card
options.description: Set the carrier's SW7 switch to external (boot from external media) and insert the SD card, then provision the dev runtime with the sd profile:
options.bootInstructions: With SW7 set to external and the SD card inserted, power on the board — it boots Avocado OS from the SD card.

options.label: eMMC (UUU)
prerequisites: Micro USB cable for the debug port
options.description: Flash the onboard eMMC over USB with the uuu-emmc profile. First put the board into serial download mode: connect the USB-C cable and the micro USB debug port to your host, set SW7 to external, and power on the board with no SD card in the slot — it comes up in serial download (UUU) mode. Then provision:

steps.content: After programming completes, set SW7 back to internal so the board boots from the onboard eMMC.

options.bootInstructions: With SW7 set to internal, power-cycle the board — it boots Avocado OS from the onboard eMMC.

serial.description: The Variscite carrier exposes a debug UART console over its onboard micro USB port — no separate TTL adapter is required. Connect the micro USB debug port to your host and open the serial device it enumerates.

jetson-agx-orin-devkit.name: NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin
jetson-agx-orin-devkit.description: The Jetson is provisioned over USB using NVIDIA's tegraflash tooling, which runs inside the Avocado SDK container.

prerequisites: NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin Developer Kit
USB-C cable (host to Jetson, for flashing)

options.label: USB recovery (tegraflash)
options.description: Provision the dev runtime using the tegraflash profile. This builds the system image and flashes it to the Jetson over USB.

steps.content: The procedure advances through several steps:

== Step 1: Signing binaries ==
...
== Step 2: Boot Jetson via RCM ==
...
== Step 3: Sending flash sequence commands ==
...

reference.label: NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin Developer Kit hardware layout

steps.text: Connect a USB-C cable from the Jetson to your host for flashing.

images.alt: USB-C port used for flashing the Jetson

steps.text: Press and hold both the Reset button and the Force Recovery button on the back of the Jetson.

images.alt: Reset button on the back of the Jetson AGX Orin Developer Kit
Force Recovery button on the back of the Jetson AGX Orin Developer Kit

steps.text: Release the Reset button. Wait 2-3 seconds, then release the Force Recovery button.

recoveryMode.verifyExpect: Look for an entry containing NVIDIA Corp. APX — this confirms the device is in recovery mode.

bootSteps: If you want a serial console, leave the Micro USB cable connected
options.bootNote: The device will boot with the provisioned system. The root user has an empty password — log in over the serial console (if connected) or via SSH once the device is on the network.

jetson-orin-nano-devkit.name: NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano
jetson-orin-nano-devkit.description: The Jetson is provisioned over USB using NVIDIA's tegraflash tooling, which runs inside the Avocado SDK container.

prerequisites: NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Developer Kit
USB-C cable for provisioning (host USB to Jetson USB-C)

options.label: USB recovery (tegraflash)
options.description: Provision the dev runtime using the tegraflash profile. This builds the system image and flashes it to the Jetson over USB.

steps.content: The procedure advances through several steps:

== Step 1: Signing binaries ==
...
== Step 2: Boot Jetson via RCM ==
...
== Step 3: Sending flash sequence commands ==
...

steps: With the device powered off, short the FC REC pin to GND using a jumper
Connect the USB-C cable from the Jetson to your host machine
recoveryMode.verifyExpect: Look for an entry containing NVIDIA Corp. APX — this confirms the device is in recovery mode.

bootSteps: Remove the jumper shorting FC REC to GND
If you attached a serial console adapter, leave it connected
options.bootNote: The device will boot with the provisioned system. The root user has an empty password — log in over the serial console (if connected) or via SSH once the device is on the network.

image.alt: Jetson Orin Nano DevKit Serial Console

options.label: SD card
options.bootInstructions: Insert the SD card into the device and apply power.

prerequisites: microSD card (8 GB+), or an NVMe SSD installed in the Pi

options.label: SD card
options.description: Insert your SD card into a reader on your host, then run the sd profile, which writes the image directly to the SD card:
options.bootInstructions: With the SD card inserted, apply power to the Raspberry Pi 5 — it boots from the card.

options.label: USB (device mode)
options.media: SD card or NVMe SSD in the Pi (flashed in place over USB)
options.description: Flash the Pi's storage in place over USB, with no card reader — this also works for an NVMe SSD installed in the Pi. With the Pi powered off and the target storage in place, press and hold the power button, then connect a USB-C data cable from the Pi to your host to apply power, releasing the button once it's connected. The Pi boots into USB device mode and your host detects it as a USB mass storage device. Note that the Pi is powered over this same USB-C cable: the Pi 5 targets a 5 V/5 A (27 W) supply and most host ports deliver less, so use a data-capable USB-C cable and a host port that can source enough current (an NVMe SSD adds to the draw); if provisioning is unreliable, use the SD-card method instead. The Pi 5's status LED confirms power: it shows red in standby and turns steady green once powered with enough current — if it won't turn green, stays red, or flickers, the supply is too weak. Then provision with the usb profile:
options.bootInstructions: Disconnect the USB-C data cable, then apply power to the Raspberry Pi 5 — it boots from its storage.

options.label: SD card
options.bootInstructions: Insert the SD card into the device and apply power.

rubikpi3.name: Thundercomm Rubik Pi 3
rubikpi3.description: The Rubik Pi 3 is provisioned by flashing the board's onboard UFS storage over USB while the device is in Qualcomm EDL (Emergency Download) mode.

images.alt: Rubik Pi 3 port and button layout (top view): Micro USB debug, USB-C, power adapter, PWR button, and EDL/recovery button
Rubik Pi 3 connector layout (isometric view): Micro USB debug, USB-C, and power adapter

prerequisites: USB-C power cable + a PD 3.0 compliant 12V/3A adapter for the power input (port 10) — bus power is not enough. The power LED only lights when PD negotiation succeeds; if it stays off the board will not boot.
USB-C (or USB-C-to-A) data cable for flashing, from the board's USB-C port (port 5) to your host
Micro-USB cable for the debug UART console (Micro-USB port, port 2) — leave this disconnected during provisioning/flashing; connect it only after provisioning succeeds

options.label: UFS (EDL)
options.description: Provision the dev runtime to the board's onboard UFS storage. The system image is flashed over the USB-C data connection (port 5) while the device is in EDL mode.

steps.content: Provisioning flashes the runtime to UFS over USB. When it completes you'll see:

reference.label: RUBIK Pi 3 — Set up your device

steps: With the board powered off, connect the USB-C data cable from the board's USB-C port (port 5) to your host.
Press and hold the EDL button (port 12) on the board.
While holding EDL, connect the power cable to the power input (port 10) to apply power, then release the button. The board enumerates in Qualcomm EDL (download) mode.
recoveryMode.verifyExpect: Look for a Qualcomm device with ID 05c6:9008 (Emergency Download mode) — this confirms the board is ready to flash.

options.bootInstructions: Disconnect the USB-C data cable (port 5), leave the power input (port 10) connected, and power-cycle the board. It boots Avocado OS from the onboard UFS storage.
options.bootNote: Now that provisioning has succeeded, connect the Micro-USB cable to the debug port (port 2) for the UART console (see Serial Console above). The root user is passwordless in the dev runtime.

serial.description: The Rubik Pi 3 exposes its debug UART over the onboard Micro-USB port (port 2) — no TTL adapter is required. Connect the Micro-USB cable to your host only after provisioning completes (leave it disconnected while flashing). The host then enumerates a USB serial device for the console.

options.label: SD card
options.bootInstructions: Insert the SD card into the device and apply power.

options.bootInstructions: Insert the SD card into the device and apply power.
